critique.summary: The article reports that GitHub has been experiencing frequent outages and service disruptions, including issues with Actions, pull requests, notifications, and Copilot. It highlights that GitHub's uptime has fallen below 90% at times, which is significantly lower than the industry gold standard of 99.999% uptime, and notes that GitHub's SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime only for Enterprise Cloud customers.
mainPoints: GitHub experienced multiple service issues on February 9 and 10, 2026, affecting Actions, pull requests, notifications, and Copilot.
Notification delays of up to 50 minutes were reported, and Copilot faced policy propagation problems preventing access to new models.
GitHub's public status page was changed, making it harder to assess overall uptime over the past 90 days.
Unofficial reconstructed status data suggests GitHub's uptime dropped below 90% at one point in 2025.
GitHub's SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime only for Enterprise Cloud customers, not all users.
The article contextualizes GitHub's issues within a broader trend of cloud service instability, noting that some vendors struggle to maintain even 90% uptime.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about GitHub's outages and service disruptions are consistent with publicly available incident reports and status updates from GitHub around the dates mentioned. GitHub's SLA for Enterprise Cloud customers does specify 99.9% uptime, and it is known that public status pages sometimes change in format, affecting transparency. The assertion that uptime dropped below 90% in 2025 is based on unofficial reconstructed data and should be treated cautiously. Overall, the article's factual claims about outages and uptime challenges are accurate, but some data relies on unofficial sources.
considerations: The article does not provide direct official statements or multiple sources confirming the exact uptime percentages or outage durations beyond GitHub's own status updates.
It does not explore the technical reasons behind the outages or GitHub's plans to improve reliability.
The impact of these outages on different user segments (e.g., free vs. enterprise users) is not detailed.
No perspectives from GitHub or Microsoft representatives are included to provide context or mitigation efforts.
The article does not compare GitHub's uptime with other major cloud or code hosting providers in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around GitHub's recent reliability and availability issues, with many users criticizing its uptime and security flaws, especially after its migration to Azure. Some comments provide nuanced views on the complexity of maintaining such a large platform, while others express frustration and suggest alternatives or decentralization.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
While GitHub obsess over shoving AI into everything, the rest of the platform is genuinely crumbling and its security flaws are being abused to cause massive damage. Last week Aqua Security was breached and a few repositories it owns were infected. The threat actors abused widespread use of mutable references in GitHub Actions, which the community has been screaming about for years, to infect potentially thousands of CI runs. They also abused an issue GitHub has acknowledged but refused to fix that allows smuggling malicious Action references into workflows that look harmless.

GHA can’t even be called Swiss cheese anymore, it’s so much worse than that. Major overhauls are needed. The best we’ve got is Immutable Releases which are opt in on a per-repository basis.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights specific security issues with GitHub Actions that have been publicly acknowledged, making it a well-informed and accurate critique.

comments.author: 827a
comments.text:
I don't want to give too much credit to Github, because their uptime is truly horrendous and they need to fix it. But: I've felt like its a little unfair to judge the uptime of company platforms like this; by saying "if any feature at all is down, its all down" and then translating that into 9s for the platform.

I never use Github Copilot; it does go down a lot, if their status page is to be believed; I don't really care when it goes down, because it going down doesn't bring down the rest of Github. I care about Github's uptime ignoring Copilot. Everyone's slice of what they care about is a little different, so the only correct way to speak on Github's uptime is to be precise and probably focus on a lot of the core stuff that tons of people care about and that's been struggling lately: Core git operations, website functionality, api access, actions, etc.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a nuanced perspective on uptime metrics, emphasizing the importance of distinguishing between core and peripheral services.

comments.author: dijit
comments.text:
I’m surprised it’s even as high as three nines, at one point in 2025 it was below 90%; not even a single nine.[0] (which, to be fair includes co-pilot, which is the worst of availabilities).

People on lobsters a month ago were congratulating Github on achieving a single nine of uptime.[1]

I make jokes about putting all our eggs in one basket under the guise of “nobody got fired for buying x; but there are sure a lot of unemployed people”- but I think there’s an insidious conversation that always used to erupt:

“Hey, take it easy on them, it’s super hard to do ops at this scale”.

Which lands hard on my ears when the normal argument in favour of centralising everything is that “you can’t hope to run things as good as they do, since there’s economies of scale”.

These two things can’t be true simultaneously.. this is the evidence.

[0]: https://mrshu.github.io/github-statuses/

[1]: https://lobste.rs/s/00edzp/missing_github_status_page#c_3cxe...
comments.analysis: This comment references historical uptime data and challenges common assumptions about GitHub's reliability, supported by external data sources.

comments.author: embedding-shape
comments.text:
From GitHub CTO in 2025 when they announced they're moving everything to Azure instead of letting GitHub's infrastructure remain independent:

> For us, availability is job #1, and this migration ensures GitHub remains the fast, reliable platform developers depend on

That went about as well as everyone thought back then.

Does anyone else remember back in ~2014-2015 sometime, when half the community was screaming at GitHub to "please be faster at adding more features"? I wish we could get back to platforms (or OSes for that matter) focusing in reliability and stability. Seems those days are long gone.
comments.analysis: The comment contextualizes the migration's impact on reliability, reflecting community sentiment and historical corporate decisions.

comments.author: mikeve
comments.text: Just to add a little bit of nuance to this not because I'm trying to defend GitHub, they definitely need to up their reliability, but the 90% uptime figure represents every single service that GitHub offers being online 90% of the time. You don't need every single service to be online in order to use GitHub. For example, I don't use Copilot myself and it's seen a 96.47% uptime, the worst of the services which are tracked.
comments.analysis: This adds nuance to uptime statistics by clarifying that aggregated uptime across all services may not reflect user experience with core features.

comments.author: ChrisArchitect
comments.text:
Feb 10th post OP;

More recently:

Addressing GitHub's recent availability issues

https://github.blog/news-insights/company-news/addressing-gi...

(with a smattering of submissions here the last few weeks but no discussion)
comments.analysis: References an official communication from GitHub, providing a factual update relevant to the discussion.

comments.author: BANRONFANTHE
comments.text:
For solo and small team projects, I've started treating GitHub as distribution rather than infrastructure. Git itself is distributed — the repo on my machine is the source of truth. Deploy scripts that can run without GitHub Actions. Local backups of anything critical.
It's a bit more work upfront, but the peace of mind when you see yet another incident on the status page is worth it.
comments.analysis: Offers practical advice to reduce dependency on GitHub uptime, reflecting a realistic approach to platform instability.

comments.author: dathinab
comments.text:
wait they still have 3 ninth, it really doesn't feel like that

but then their status center isn't really trust-able anymore and a lot of temporary issues I have been running into seem to be temporary, partial, localized failures which sometimes fall under temp. slow to a point of usability. Temporary served outdated (by >30min) main/head. etc.

so that won't even show up in this statistics
comments.analysis: Highlights limitations in status reporting and uptime metrics, suggesting that real-world experience may be worse than reported.

comments.author: DailyGeo
comments.text:
The availability expectations gap is interesting 
from an education standpoint. Students are tought
that 99.9% sounds impressive without contextualizing 
what that means in practice — roughly 8 hours of 
downtime per year. For a platform that millions 
of developers depend on as critical infrastructure 
during work hours, that math hits very differently 
than it does for a consumer app.
comments.analysis: Provides useful context on uptime expectations and their practical implications for critical infrastructure like GitHub.

comments.author: pscanf
comments.text:
I only use GitHub (and actions) for personal open-source projects, so I can't really complain because I'm getting everything for free¹. But even for those projects I recently had to (partially) switch actions to a paid solution² because GitHub's runners were randomly getting stuck for no discernible reason.

¹ Glossing over the "what they're getting in return" part. ² https://www.warpbuild.com/
comments.analysis: Shares firsthand experience of reliability issues with GitHub Actions, supporting broader claims of instability.

comments.author: yifanl
comments.text: https://mrshu.github.io/github-statuses/ Even ignoring Copilot, they seem to be barely at 2 nines of uptime for any service component.
comments.analysis: Supports the argument that GitHub's overall uptime is worse than advertised, referencing external uptime tracking.

comments.author: _heimdall
comments.text:
I'm surprised GitHub got by acting fairly independently inside Microsoft for so long. I'm also surprised GitHub employees expected that to last

The real problem today IMO is that Microsoft waited so long to drop the charade that they now felt like they had to rip the bandaid. From what I've heard the transition hasn't gone very smoothly at all, and they've mostly been given tight deadlines with little to no help from Microsoft counterparts.
comments.analysis: Provides insider-like perspective on organizational challenges affecting GitHub's stability post-acquisition.

comments.author: neonihil
comments.text: Nothing unexpected. Microsoft has a remarkable talent for turning good products into useless ones. Skype is another good showcase of such talent.
comments.analysis: Expresses a common sentiment about Microsoft acquisitions, though somewhat generalized and emotional.

comments.author: lijok
comments.text: ITT lots of complaining, not much building. Microsoft does not give a fuck what you think - they only care if the revenue line goes up. And the revenue line keeps going up despite this instability. Want to build the next unicorn? Build a GitHub competitor.
comments.analysis: Reflects a cynical but realistic view of corporate priorities, though lacks constructive detail.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: It's time to look for a decentralized Non-Hub alternative.
comments.analysis: Proposes decentralization as a solution, though without detailed reasoning or evidence.
